title: When You Stop Pretending to Be Spiritual
dek: *The moment you stop performing spirituality is the moment you can actually begin. Pretending drains you. Authenticity—even when it's messy, angry, and impatient—feeds something older and truer than any Instagram story about your morning ritual ever could.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Spiritual performance drains the same energy that authentic practice restores. When you stop pretending, anger becomes a diagnostic tool instead of proof you're failing. The body knows the difference between performance and presence, and it will tell you through sensation.

Most people think anger means they've lost their spiritual progress, like a video game character dropping back to level one. But what if anger is actually the sound of something true trying to break through a performance you've been giving for so long you forgot it was a show? You know the performance. The careful breath before you speak. The soft voice you've cultivated. The way you pause and say "I'm just sitting with that" when really you want to throw the ceramic mug across the room and watch it shatter against something hard. The performance is exhausting because it requires constant monitoring, a background program running at all times, checking: Am I being spiritual enough right now? Would my teacher approve? What would someone on the path do in this moment? In my own practice, the breaking point came on a Tuesday. Nothing dramatic. I was making tea and dropped the honey jar. It cracked, and honey spread across the counter in a slow, sticky pool. And instead of breathing through it or finding the lesson or thanking the universe for the opportunity to practice patience, I said "fuck" out loud, with my whole chest. Then I laughed. Because it felt like the first honest thing I'd done in months. ## The Metabolic Cost of Performance Your body knows when you're performing. It registers the gap between what you feel and what you show as a threat, a kind of chronic low-level danger that never quite resolves. Robert Augustus Masters, in his work on spiritual bypassing, points out that using spiritual concepts to avoid uncomfortable feelings doesn't make those feelings disappear - it drives them underground where they ferment and gain power. The physiological cost is real. When you suppress anger, your cardiovascular system doesn't get the memo that you're being "spiritual" about it. Research shows that PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load demonstrates measurable increases in blood pressure and heart rate variability even when anger is consciously suppressed. Your body is still running the anger program; you've just muted the speakers. This is different from transformation. Transformation means meeting the anger, feeling it in your jaw and your fists and your throat, and then choosing what to do with that energy. Performance means smiling while your blood pressure climbs, while your digestive system shuts down, while some part of you that still remembers how to be a mammal wonders why you won't let it protect you from what feels threatening. The performance requires fuel. It burns through magnesium, B vitamins, and the same neurotransmitter reserves that help you sleep and feel joy. Over the years I've seen people come to [the about page](/about) of this site looking for herbs to fix their anxiety, their insomnia, their digestive problems - and what they're often dealing with is the accumulated debt of pretending to be calm when they're not. ## What Anger Actually Tells You Harriet Lerner, in her research on anger and relationships, makes the point that anger is often the first signal that something in your life is out of alignment with your actual values. It's diagnostic. It points. This boundary was crossed. This need wasn't met. This promise was broken. Anger says: pay attention here. But when you're performing spirituality, you've trained yourself to ignore the pointing. You've learned to reframe every boundary violation as a "lesson" and every unmet need as "attachment" and every broken promise as "letting go of expectations." Which sounds evolved until you realize you've just talked yourself out of basic self-protection. Real spiritual practice doesn't make you less angry. It makes you more honest about what you're actually feeling, which sometimes means discovering you've been furious for years and calling it "surrender." The anger was always there. You just weren't allowed to know about it because knowing about it would ruin the performance. When you stop pretending, you get to find out what the anger is actually about. Not the spiritual story you've been telling about it, but the real thing underneath. Maybe it's about the friend who takes and never gives. Maybe it's about the job that asks you to smile while it underpays you. Maybe it's about the family member who still treats you like you're twelve. The anger knows. It's been trying to tell you. You've been too busy being spiritual to listen. ## The Texture of Real Practice Here's what nobody tells you: real practice is ugly sometimes. It's crying in your car in a parking lot. It's snapping at someone you love and then having to come back and say "I was wrong, I'm sorry, I'm scared." It's sitting on your meditation cushion feeling absolutely nothing except the desire to check your phone, and then checking your phone, and then feeling bad about it, and then sitting again anyway. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that mindfulness means being present with what is, not with what you wish was there. If anger is what's there, then being present with anger is the practice. Not transcending it. Not breathing it away. Not turning it into something prettier. Just being there with the heat in your chest and the tightness in your throat and the very specific thoughts about exactly what you'd like to say to exactly whom. Your body during real practice feels different than your body during performance. Performance sits in your head, a constant narration of how you're doing. Real practice drops lower. You feel it in your belly, your pelvis, your legs. There's sensation without story. There's heat without having to explain the heat. Your breath moves differently - less controlled, more responsive to what's actually happening in your nervous system. Sometimes real practice means stopping in the middle of a yoga class because your hip is screaming and the teacher is saying "breathe into it" and you suddenly realize that "breathe into it" is just another way of saying "ignore your body's wisdom." So you stop. You lie down. You let everyone else keep performing while you tend to what's real. ## The Practice: Five Minutes of Non-Spiritual Honesty Find a place where no one can hear you. Set a timer for five minutes. Say out loud, in whatever voice comes, everything you're not supposed to feel. Not the processed version. Not the spiritually acceptable version. The raw thing. "I'm angry that - " "I hate when - " "I want to - " "I'm so tired of - " Let it be ugly. Let it be petty. Let it be the opposite of every wise thing you've ever said in public. Let your voice get loud or sharp or shaky. Let your body move however it wants to move. Clench your fists. Stamp your feet. Make faces in the mirror. This isn't catharsis. It's data collection. You're finding out what's actually in there underneath the performance. Some of it will surprise you. Some of it you've known for years but never said in words. The timer will go off. You'll stop. And something in your nervous system will have shifted because for five minutes you told the truth without trying to evolve it first. ## When the Performance Breaks Down The breakdown usually comes in stages. First, you notice you're tired. Not the kind of tired that sleep fixes, but a bone-deep exhaustion that comes from constant self-monitoring. You start missing your practices. The meditation cushion gathers dust. You forget to sage the apartment. You eat the non-organic vegetables and don't feel guilty. Then comes the guilt about not feeling guilty. You think something's wrong with you. You've lost your way. You read [more articles in this journal](/articles) looking for the thing that will get you back on track. But what if there's no track? What if the exhaustion is your body's way of saying: the performance is costing more than it's worth? In our experience, the breakdown is actually a breakthrough trying to happen. It's your system saying: I can't keep running these two programs simultaneously anymore - the feeling program and the spiritual-performance program. One of them has to go. And it's never going to be the feeling program, because feelings are older and more fundamental than any spiritual framework you learned at a workshop. The breakdown might look like snapping at your partner over something small and then, instead of immediately apologizing and processing and taking responsibility, just sitting there in the anger, feeling how much you mean it. It might look like skipping the group meditation because you'd rather watch television and eat cheese. It might look like saying "I don't know" when someone asks you what you're learning right now, because the honest answer is "nothing, I'm just trying to get through the week." Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body, explored in depth in [Waking the Tiger - Peter Levine](https://www.amazon.com/dp/155643943X?tag=spankyspinola-20), shows how the body stores what we don't allow ourselves to express. The performance creates storage. The breakdown is when the storage unit gets too full and starts spilling out into your regular life, and you can't pretend it's not there anymore. ## What Opens Up After When you stop pretending to be spiritual, you get to find out what you actually are. Which is probably something more interesting than the performance ever was. You're allowed to be contradictory. You can meditate in the morning and be irritable by noon. You can love your practice and also sometimes hate it. You can believe in something larger than yourself and still want things to go your way. The people who were only interested in the performance might drift away. That's okay. They were never interested in you; they were interested in the mirror you provided for their own performance. The people who stay are the ones who can handle your reality, which includes your anger, your pettiness, your very specific opinions about things that don't matter. Your body starts to feel different. Less armored. More fluid. You might sleep better, or find that your digestion improves, or notice that your jaw isn't clenched all the time. The herbs in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) work better when you're not fighting your own nervous system. [NOW L-Tryptophan 500 mg (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0019LWV56?tag=spankyspinola-20) actually helps with sleep when you're not burning through serotonin precursors maintaining a performance. [Plum Flower Yin Chiao Chieh Tu Extract (100 tabs, 2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00CBY90EK?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support your system when you're not constantly depleting your defensive qi by pretending everything's fine. Practice becomes simpler. Not easier, but simpler. You sit because sitting helps, not because sitting makes you spiritual. You breathe because breathing feels good, not because you're supposed to. You move your body because your body wants to move, not because movement is on your self-care checklist. ## The Difference Between Spiritual and Real Here's the distinction that matters: spiritual practice, as performance, is something you do to yourself. Real practice is something you do with yourself. The first is top-down, head to body, control and improvement and fixing. The second is collaborative, a conversation between all your parts, including the ones that are angry and tired and don't want to transcend anything right now. The performance version says: I am angry, therefore I am failing. The real version says: I am angry, what is this anger trying to protect? What does it know that I've been ignoring? You can find versions of this same split on [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the focus is on divination and direct knowing rather than the cultivation work we do here. But the principle is the same: performance obscures truth, and truth is what actually helps. Spiritual communities often reward performance. They praise the people who seem calm, who speak in soft voices, who never seem ruffled. But calmness isn't the same as peace, and a soft voice isn't the same as kindness. Sometimes the kindest thing is to be loud. Sometimes peace comes after you've let yourself be ruffled, after you've felt the full force of what you actually feel. ## Three Questions People Actually Ask ### If I stop performing spirituality, won't I just become an angry person who justifies bad behavior? There's a difference between expressing anger and dumping it on people. The performance taught you to suppress; the work now is to learn discernment. You can feel angry and choose not to send the email. You can notice the heat in your chest and decide this isn't the moment to speak. But the choice comes from awareness, not from a rule about what spiritual people do. You're not justifying bad behavior; you're developing actual emotional literacy instead of performing emotional transcendence. ### How do I know if I'm being authentic or just being reactive? Your body knows. Reactive feels like an explosion, a sudden discharge you can't quite control. Authentic feels like weight, like substance, like something that's been true for a while and is now finding words. Reactive happens fast and burns out fast. Authentic moves slower and lasts longer. If you're not sure, wait. Sit with it. See if it's still there tomorrow. Reactive anger evaporates. Real anger stays because it's pointing at something that hasn't been addressed. ### What if I've built my whole identity around being the calm, spiritual one? Then you get to grieve that identity, which is real grief, and you get to find out who you are without it. The people who loved the performance might be confused or disappointed. Some relationships might not survive the transition. But the relationships that do survive will be based on reality instead of performance, which means they'll actually nourish you instead of drain you. You're not losing yourself; you're finding yourself underneath the costume. It's uncomfortable. It's also worth it. ## What Happens Next You'll probably swing too far the other way at first. You'll be reactive when you meant to be boundaried. You'll say things you wish you'd said differently. You'll confuse honesty with cruelty sometimes, or mistake performance for authenticity because the habits run deep. That's fine. That's the learning. Over time, you'll develop something more interesting than either the performance or the reaction. You'll develop discernment, which is the ability to feel everything and choose consciously what to do with what you feel. You'll have access to your anger and your tenderness, your impatience and your patience, your selfishness and your generosity. You'll be whole instead of holy, which turns out to be more useful. The spiritual performance promised transcendence. What you get instead is integration. All your parts at the table. All your voices allowed to speak. The angry one and the frightened one and the tender one and the one who just wants to rest. They're all you. They were always you. You just couldn't afford to know them while you were busy being spiritual. And here's what nobody tells you until you get there: being whole is more sacred than being spiritual ever was. The mess is holy. The contradiction is holy. The anger that finally gets to speak after years of silence is so holy it'll break your heart open in ways that a thousand perfect meditations never could. You don't transcend your humanity. You arrive in it, fully, for the first time. And that arrival, messy and imperfect and real, is what practice was always actually for. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on when you stop pretending to be spiritual, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
